title: Horse riding

infobox.value: (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *equester*, *equestr-*, *equus*, 'horseman', 'horse'),[[2]](#cite_note-2) commonly known as **horse riding** ([Commonwealth English](/wiki/Commonwealth_English "Commonwealth English")) or **horseback riding** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) includes the disciplines of riding, [driving](/wiki/Driving_(horse) "Driving (horse)"), and [vaulting](/wiki/Equestrian_vaulting "Equestrian vaulting"). This broad description includes the use of [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse") for practical [working](/wiki/Working_animal "Working animal") purposes, transportation, recreational activities, artistic or cultural exercises, and [competitive sport](/wiki/Animals_in_sport "Animals in sport").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Horse riding](/w/index.php?title=Horse_riding&redirect=no "Horse riding"))

"Horseback", "horse riding", "horsewoman", and "equestrians" redirect here. For the back of a horse, see [Back (horse)](/wiki/Back_(horse) "Back (horse)"). For other uses, see [Horse riding (disambiguation)](/wiki/Horse_riding_(disambiguation) "Horse riding (disambiguation)"), [Horsewoman (disambiguation)](/wiki/Horsewoman_(disambiguation) "Horsewoman (disambiguation)"), and [Equestrian (disambiguation)](/wiki/Equestrian_(disambiguation) "Equestrian (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Horse_riding_in_coca_cola_arena_-_melbourne_show_2005.jpg/250px-Horse_riding_in_coca_cola_arena_-_melbourne_show_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Horse_riding_in_coca_cola_arena_-_melbourne_show_2005.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Queluz_Palace_horses_approach_%289180978620%29.jpg/250px-Queluz_Palace_horses_approach_%289180978620%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queluz_Palace_horses_approach_(9180978620).jpg)

[Lusitano](/wiki/Lusitano "Lusitano") riders of the [Portuguese School of Equestrian Art](/wiki/Portuguese_School_of_Equestrian_Art "Portuguese School of Equestrian Art"), one of the "Big Four" most prestigious [riding academies](/wiki/Category:Equestrian_educational_establishments "Category:Equestrian educational establishments") in the world, alongside the [Cadre Noir](/wiki/Cadre_Noir "Cadre Noir"), the [Spanish Riding School](/wiki/Spanish_Riding_School "Spanish Riding School"), and the [Royal Andalusian School](/wiki/Royal_Andalusian_School_of_Equestrian_Art "Royal Andalusian School of Equestrian Art").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/10_Iceland_tourism_-_Icelandic_horses_ride_in_Iceland%2C_horseback_riding_tourists.jpg/250px-10_Iceland_tourism_-_Icelandic_horses_ride_in_Iceland%2C_horseback_riding_tourists.jpg)](/wiki/File:10_Iceland_tourism_-_Icelandic_horses_ride_in_Iceland,_horseback_riding_tourists.jpg)

Equestrian tour on traditional local breed, [Icelandic horses](/wiki/Icelandic_horse "Icelandic horse") in [Skaftafell](/wiki/Skaftafell "Skaftafell") mountains of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland")

**Equestrianism** (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *equester*, *equestr-*, *equus*, 'horseman', 'horse'),[[2]](#cite_note-2) commonly known as **horse riding** ([Commonwealth English](/wiki/Commonwealth_English "Commonwealth English")) or **horseback riding** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) includes the disciplines of riding, [driving](/wiki/Driving_(horse) "Driving (horse)"), and [vaulting](/wiki/Equestrian_vaulting "Equestrian vaulting"). This broad description includes the use of [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse") for practical [working](/wiki/Working_animal "Working animal") purposes, transportation, recreational activities, artistic or cultural exercises, and [competitive sport](/wiki/Animals_in_sport "Animals in sport").

However, recent studies have raised serious concerns about the impact of riding on horse welfare and pain (see: [Impact of Riding on Horse Welfare](#Impact_of_Riding_on_Horse_Welfare)).

## Overview of equestrian activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview of equestrian activities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Gansu_Museum_2007_308.jpg/250px-Gansu_Museum_2007_308.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gansu_Museum_2007_308.jpg)

Musicians riding horses, [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty")

Horses are [trained](/wiki/Horse_training "Horse training") and ridden for practical working purposes, such as in [police work](/wiki/Mounted_police "Mounted police") or for controlling herd animals on a [ranch](/wiki/Ranch "Ranch"). They are also used in [competitive sports](/wiki/Horse#Sport "Horse") including [dressage](/wiki/Dressage "Dressage"), [endurance riding](/wiki/Endurance_riding "Endurance riding"), [eventing](/wiki/Eventing "Eventing"), [reining](/wiki/Reining "Reining"), [show jumping](/wiki/Show_jumping "Show jumping"), [tent pegging](/wiki/Tent_pegging "Tent pegging"), [vaulting](/wiki/Equestrian_vaulting "Equestrian vaulting"), [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo"), [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing"), [driving](/wiki/Driving_(horse) "Driving (horse)"), and [rodeo](/wiki/Rodeo "Rodeo") (see additional equestrian sports listed later in this article for more examples). Some popular forms of competition are grouped together at [horse shows](/wiki/Horse_show "Horse show") where horses perform in a wide variety of disciplines. Horses (and other [equids](/wiki/Equid "Equid") such as [mules](/wiki/Mule "Mule")) are used for non-competitive recreational riding, such as [fox hunting](/wiki/Fox_hunting "Fox hunting"), [trail riding](/wiki/Trail_riding "Trail riding"), or [hacking](/wiki/Pleasure_riding "Pleasure riding"). There is public access to horse trails in almost every part of the world; many parks, [ranches](/wiki/Ranch "Ranch"), and public [stables](/wiki/Stable "Stable") offer both guided and independent riding. Horses are also used for [therapeutic](/wiki/Therapeutic_horseback_riding "Therapeutic horseback riding") purposes both in specialized para-equestrian competition as well as non-competitive riding to improve human health and emotional development.

[Horses](/wiki/Horses "Horses") are also [driven](/wiki/Driving_(horse) "Driving (horse)") in [harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing"), at [horse shows](/wiki/Horse_show "Horse show"), and in other types of exhibition such as [historical reenactment](/wiki/Historical_reenactment "Historical reenactment") or ceremony, often pulling [carriages](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage"). In some parts of the world, they are still used for practical purposes such as [farming](/wiki/Farm "Farm").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Horses continue to be used in public service, in traditional ceremonies (parades, funerals), [police](/wiki/Police_horse "Police horse") and volunteer mounted patrols and for [mounted search and rescue](/wiki/Mounted_search_and_rescue "Mounted search and rescue").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Riding halls](/wiki/Riding_hall "Riding hall"), also known as indoor arenas or schools, enable training of horse and rider in all weathers as well as indoor competition riding.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History of horse use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History of horse use")]

Main articles: [Domestication of the horse](/wiki/Domestication_of_the_horse "Domestication of the horse") and [Horses in warfare](/wiki/Horses_in_warfare "Horses in warfare")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Cave_painting_in_Doushe_cave%2C_Lorstan%2C_Iran%2C_8th_millennium_BC.JPG/250px-Cave_painting_in_Doushe_cave%2C_Lorstan%2C_Iran%2C_8th_millennium_BC.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cave_painting_in_Doushe_cave,_Lorstan,_Iran,_8th_millennium_BC.JPG)

Prehistoric cave painting, depicting a horse and rider

Though there is controversy over the exact date horses were [domesticated](/wiki/Domestication "Domestication") and when they were first ridden, the best estimate is that horses first were ridden approximately 3500 BC. There is some evidence that about 3000 BC, near the [Dnieper River](/wiki/Dnieper_River "Dnieper River") and the [Don River](/wiki/Don_River_(Russia) "Don River (Russia)"), people were using [bits](/wiki/Bit_(horse) "Bit (horse)") on horses, as a [stallion](/wiki/Stallion "Stallion") that was buried there shows teeth wear consistent with using a bit.[[7]](#cite_note-Chamberlin69-7) However, the most unequivocal early [archaeological](/wiki/Archeology "Archeology") evidence of equines put to working use was of horses being driven. [Chariot](/wiki/Chariot "Chariot") burials about 2500 BC present the most direct hard evidence of horses used as [working animals](/wiki/Working_animal "Working animal"). In ancient times chariot warfare was followed by the use of [war horses](/wiki/Horses_in_warfare "Horses in warfare") as light and heavy [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry"). The horse played an important role throughout human history all over the world, both in warfare and in peaceful pursuits such as [transportation](/wiki/Transportation "Transportation"), [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade") and [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"). Horses lived in North America, but died out at the end of the [Ice Age](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"). Horses were brought back to North America by European explorers, beginning with the second voyage of [Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") in 1493.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Equestrianism was introduced in the [1900 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1900_Summer_Olympics "1900 Summer Olympics") as an Olympic sport with jumping events.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Horse racing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Horse racing")]

Main articles: [Horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing"), [Harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing"), and [Parimutuel gambling](/wiki/Parimutuel_gambling "Parimutuel gambling")

Humans appear to have long expressed a desire to know which horse or horses were the fastest, and [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") has ancient roots. [Gambling](/wiki/Gambling "Gambling") on horse races appears to go hand-in hand with racing and has a long history as well. [Thoroughbreds](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") have the pre-eminent reputation as a racing breed, but other breeds also race.

### Types of horse racing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Types of horse racing")]

Under saddle:

* [Thoroughbred horse racing](/wiki/Thoroughbred_horse_racing "Thoroughbred horse racing") is the most popular form worldwide. In the UK, it is known as [flat racing](/wiki/Flat_racing "Flat racing") and is governed by the [Jockey Club](/wiki/Jockey_Club "Jockey Club") in the United Kingdom. In the US, horse racing is governed by the [Jockey Club](/wiki/Jockey_Club_(United_States) "Jockey Club (United States)"). Other light breeds are also raced worldwide.
* [Steeplechasing](/wiki/Steeplechase_(horse_racing) "Steeplechase (horse racing)") involves racing on a track where the horses also jump over obstacles. It is most common in the UK, where it is also called [National Hunt](/wiki/National_Hunt "National Hunt") racing.

In harness:

* Both light and heavy breeds as well as ponies are raced in harness with a [sulky](/wiki/Sulky "Sulky") or racing bike. The [Standardbred](/wiki/Standardbred "Standardbred") dominates the sport in both [trotting](/wiki/Trot_(horse_gait) "Trot (horse gait)") and [pacing](/wiki/Pacing_(horse_gait) "Pacing (horse gait)") varieties.
* The United States Trotting Association organizes [harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing") in the United States.
* Harness racing is also found throughout Europe, New Zealand and Australia.

Distance racing:

* [Endurance riding](/wiki/Endurance_riding "Endurance riding"), takes place over a given, measured distance and the horses have an even start. Top level races are usually 50 to 100 miles (80 to 161 km), over mountainous or other natural terrain, with scheduled stops to take the horses' vital signs, check soundness and verify that the horse is fit to continue. The first horse to finish and be confirmed by the veterinarian as fit to continue is the winner. Limited distance rides of about 20–25 miles (32–40 km) are offered to newcomers. Variants include [Ride and Tie](/wiki/Ride_and_Tie "Ride and Tie") and various forms of [long riding](/w/index.php?title=Long_riding&action=edit&redlink=1 "Long riding (page does not exist)").[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

## International and Olympic disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: International and Olympic disciplines")]

Main articles: [Equestrian events at the Summer Olympics](/wiki/Equestrian_events_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian events at the Summer Olympics") and [International Federation for Equestrian Sports](/wiki/International_Federation_for_Equestrian_Sports "International Federation for Equestrian Sports")

Equestrian events were first included in the modern [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") in 1900. By 1912, all three Olympic disciplines were part of the games. Three forms of competition are recognized worldwide and are a part of the [equestrian events](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the Summer Olympics") at the Olympics. They are governed by the rules of the [International Federation for Equestrian Sports](/wiki/International_Federation_for_Equestrian_Sports "International Federation for Equestrian Sports") (FEI):

* [Dressage](/wiki/Dressage "Dressage") ("training" in [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")) involves the progressive training of the horse to a high level of [impulsion](/wiki/Impulsion "Impulsion"), [collection](/wiki/Collection_(horse) "Collection (horse)") and obedience.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Competitive dressage has the goal of showing the horse carrying out, on request, the natural movements that it performs without thinking while running loose.
* [Show jumping](/wiki/Show_jumping "Show jumping") comprises a timed event judged on the ability of the horse and rider to jump over a series of obstacles, in a given order and with the fewest refusals or knockdowns of portions of the obstacles.
* [Eventing](/wiki/Eventing "Eventing"), also called *combined training*, *horse trials*, the *three-day event*, *the Military* or *the complete test*, puts together the obedience of dressage with the athletic ability of show jumping, the fitness demands the [cross-country jumping](/wiki/Cross-country_equestrianism "Cross-country equestrianism") phase. In the last-named, the horses jump over fixed obstacles, such as logs, stone walls, [banks](/wiki/Bank_(obstacle) "Bank (obstacle)"), [ditches](/wiki/Ditch_(obstacle) "Ditch (obstacle)") and [water](/wiki/Water_(obstacle) "Water (obstacle)"), trying to finish the course under the "optimum time". There was formerly a 'Steeple Chase' phase, which has been excluded from most major competitions to bring them in line with the Olympic standard.

The additional internationally sanctioned but non-Olympic disciplines governed by the [FEI](/wiki/International_Federation_for_Equestrian_Sports "International Federation for Equestrian Sports") are: [combined driving](/wiki/Combined_driving "Combined driving"); [endurance](/wiki/Endurance_riding "Endurance riding"); [reining](/wiki/Reining "Reining"); and [vaulting](/wiki/Equestrian_vaulting "Equestrian vaulting"). These disciplines are part of the [FEI World Equestrian Games](/wiki/FEI_World_Equestrian_Games "FEI World Equestrian Games") every four years and may hold their own individual World Championships in other years. The FEI also recognizes [horseball](/wiki/Horseball "Horseball") and [tent pegging](/wiki/Tent_pegging "Tent pegging") as its two regional disciplines.

### Para-equestrian disciplines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Para-equestrian disciplines")]

[Para-equestrian](/wiki/Para-equestrian "Para-equestrian") competition at the international level, including the [Paralympics](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_Summer_Paralympics "Equestrian at the Summer Paralympics"), are also governed by the FEI and offer the following competition events:

* Para-Equestrian Dressage is conducted under the same rules as conventional Dressage, but with riders divided into different competition grades based on their functional abilities.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* Para-Equestrian Driving places competitors in grades based on their skill.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Impact of riding on horse welfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Impact of riding on horse welfare")]

Several studies have raised concerns about the effects of horseback riding on horses.

A British study examined lameness and pain in both sport and school horses whose owners reported no such issues. Despite the owners’ reports, the examination showed that about 73% of the horses exhibited some degree of lameness, and about half of them showed signs of pain during riding.[[14]](#cite_note-Dyson2020Animals-14) Pain was measured using the Ridden Horse Pain Ethogram (RHpE).[[15]](#cite_note-Dyson2018-15)[[16]](#cite_note-DysonBOBOEquine-16) Similar results have been found in other studies and locations.[[17]](#cite_note-Greve2014-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Rhodin2016-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Egenvall2010-19) Similar issues have also been found in leisure riding horses.[[20]](#cite_note-Visser2014-20)

The impact of riding on horses depends on daily riding duration. Riding school horses show more facial grimacing, according to the Horse Grimace Scale, on days of moderate work (1 to 2 hours of riding) than on rest days, indicating pain or discomfort. Pain indicators increase further on days of prolonged work (3 to 4 hours).[[21]](#cite_note-Ijichi2023-21)

Although rider skill positively affects horse welfare, it was found that all riders, regardless of skill or experience, fail to recognize behavioral signs that indicate pain.[[14]](#cite_note-Dyson2020Animals-14) The researchers note that educating riders to recognize behaviors indicating pain may allow earlier detection of problems in the horse and provide appropriate treatment. Indeed, learning the RHpE improved veterinarians’ ability to identify musculoskeletal pain in horses significantly.[[22]](#cite_note-DysonBOBOEquine2-22)

In addition, it was found that riders struggle to recognize proper saddle fit or suitable saddle shape, regardless of their skill level.[[23]](#cite_note-Douglas2025-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Dittmann2021-24) Various saddle and fitting issues can cause back pain. Harmful problems documented include asymmetrical saddle panels and a poorly fitted tree head (too narrow or too wide).[[24]](#cite_note-Dittmann2021-24) The Society of Master Saddlers has published guidelines for optimal saddle fitting.[[25]](#cite_note-MasterSaddlersAssociation-25) Researchers have highlighted the importance of regular saddle fitting checks by a qualified fitter.[[23]](#cite_note-Douglas2025-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Dittmann2021-24)

However, even among qualified Society of Master Saddlers fitters, agreement on saddle assessments was not complete, so fitting a saddle according to the guidelines does not guarantee protection against errors.[[26]](#cite_note-Guire2017-26)

It was found that the rider’s weight affects the horse’s pain.[[27]](#cite_note-Dyson2020influence-27) In addition, weight distribution on the horse’s back also seems to be important. It is estimated that a skilled rider has better stability and weight distribution, which eases strain on the horse’s back.[[24]](#cite_note-Dittmann2021-24)

## Haute École

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Haute École")]

Main article: [Classical dressage](/wiki/Classical_dressage "Classical dressage")

The *haute école* ([F.](/wiki/French_language "French language") "high school"), an advanced component of [Classical dressage](/wiki/Classical_dressage "Classical dressage"), is a highly refined set of skills seldom used in competition but often seen in demonstration performances.

The world's leading Classical dressage programs include:

* The [Cadre Noir](/wiki/Cadre_Noir "Cadre Noir") in [Saumur](/wiki/Saumur "Saumur"), [France](/wiki/France "France").
* The [Spanish Riding School](/wiki/Spanish_Riding_School "Spanish Riding School") in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria").
* The [Portuguese School of Equestrian Art](/wiki/Portuguese_School_of_Equestrian_Art "Portuguese School of Equestrian Art") at [Queluz National Palace](/wiki/Queluz_National_Palace "Queluz National Palace"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal").
* The [Royal Andalusian School of Equestrian Art](/wiki/Royal_Andalusian_School_of_Equestrian_Art "Royal Andalusian School of Equestrian Art") in [Jerez de la Frontera](/wiki/Jerez_de_la_Frontera "Jerez de la Frontera"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").

Other major classical teams include the [South African Lipizzaners](/wiki/South_African_Lipizzaners "South African Lipizzaners") and the [Hollandsche Manege](/wiki/Hollandsche_Manege "Hollandsche Manege") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").

## Horse shows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Horse shows")]

Main article: [Horse show](/wiki/Horse_show "Horse show")

[Horse shows](/wiki/Horse_show "Horse show") are held throughout the world with a tremendous variety of possible events, equipment, attire, and judging standards used. However, most forms of horse show competition can be broken into the following broad categories:

* [Equitation](/wiki/Equitation "Equitation"), sometimes called *seat and hands* or *horsemanship*, refers to events where the rider is judged on form, style and ability.
* *Pleasure, flat* or *under saddle* classes feature horses who are ridden on the flat (not jumped) and judged on manners, performance, movement, style and quality.
* *Halter, in-hand breeding* or *conformation* classes, where the horse is led by a handler on the ground and judged on conformation and suitability as a breeding animal.
* *Harness* classes, where the horse is [driven](/wiki/Driving_(horse) "Driving (horse)") rather than ridden, but still judged on manners, performance and quality.
* *Jumping* or *Over Fences* refers broadly to both [show jumping](/wiki/Show_jumping "Show jumping") and [show hunter](/wiki/Show_hunter "Show hunter"), where horses and riders must jump obstacles.

## English riding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: English riding")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/WEG_2010_-_Dressage_Qualifying.jpg/250px-WEG_2010_-_Dressage_Qualifying.jpg)](/wiki/File:WEG_2010_-_Dressage_Qualifying.jpg)

A dressage rider

Main article: [English riding](/wiki/English_riding "English riding")

In addition to the classical Olympic events, the following forms of competition are seen. In North America they are referred to as "[English riding](/wiki/English_riding "English riding")" in contrast with western riding; elsewhere in the world, if a distinction is necessary, they are usually described as "classic riding":

* [Hunt seat](/wiki/Hunt_seat "Hunt seat") or [Hunter](/wiki/Show_hunter "Show hunter") classes judge the movement and the form of horses suitable for work over fences. A typical [show hunter](/wiki/Show_hunter "Show hunter") division would include classes over fences as well as "Hunter under Saddle" or "flat" classes (sometimes called "hack" classes), in which the horse is judged on its performance, manners and movement without having to jump. Hunters have a long, flat-kneed trot, sometimes called "daisy cutter" movement, a phrase suggesting a good hunter could slice daisies in a field when it reaches its stride out. The over fences classes in [show hunter](/wiki/Show_hunter "Show hunter") competition are judged on the form of the horse, its manners and the smoothness of the course. A horse with good jumping form snaps its knees up and jumps with a good [bascule](/wiki/Bascule_(horse) "Bascule (horse)"). It should also be able to canter or gallop with control while having a stride long enough to make a proper number of strides over a given distance between fences. Hunter classes differ from jumper classes, in which they are not timed, and equitation classes, in which the rider's performance is the focus. Hunter style is based on fox hunting, so jumps in the hunter division are usually more natural colors than the jumps in a jumper division.
* [Eventing](/wiki/Eventing "Eventing"), [show jumping](/wiki/Show_jumping "Show jumping") and [dressage](/wiki/Dressage "Dressage"), described under "Olympic disciplines", above are all "English" riding disciplines that in North America sometimes are loosely classified within the "hunt seat" category.
* [Saddle seat](/wiki/Saddle_seat "Saddle seat"), is a primarily American discipline, though has recently become somewhat popular in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), was created to show to best advantage the animated movement of high-stepping and gaited breeds such as the [American Saddlebred](/wiki/American_Saddlebred "American Saddlebred") and the [Tennessee Walker](/wiki/Tennessee_Walking_Horse "Tennessee Walking Horse"). [Arabians](/wiki/Arabian_horse "Arabian horse") and [Morgans](/wiki/Morgan_horse "Morgan horse") may also be shown saddle seat in the United States. There are usually three basic divisions. *Park* divisions are for the horses with the highest action. *Pleasure* divisions still emphasis animated action, but to a lesser degree, with manners ranking over animation. *Plantation* or *Country* divisions have the least amount of animation (in some breeds, the horses are flat-shod) and the greatest emphasis on manners.
* [Show hack](/wiki/Show_hack "Show hack") is a competition seen primarily in the United Kingdom, Australia and other nations influenced by British traditions, featuring horses of elegant appearance, with excellent way of going and self-carriage. A related event is [riding horse](/wiki/Riding_horse_(horse_show) "Riding horse (horse show)").

## Western riding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Western riding")]

Main article: [Western riding](/wiki/Western_riding "Western riding")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Quarter_Horse_trotting.jpg/250px-Quarter_Horse_trotting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quarter_Horse_trotting.jpg)

Western horsemanship attire and style of riding

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Apartacao.jpg/250px-Apartacao.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apartacao.jpg)

[Cutting horse](/wiki/Cutting_horse "Cutting horse") competition.

[Western riding](/wiki/Western_riding "Western riding") evolved from the cattle-working and warfare traditions brought to the Americas by the [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), and both equipment and riding style evolved to meet the working needs of the [cowboy](/wiki/Cowboy "Cowboy") on [ranches](/wiki/Ranch "Ranch") in the [American West](/wiki/American_West "American West").

The most noticeable feature of western style riding is the [western saddle](/wiki/Western_saddle "Western saddle"), which has a substantial [saddle tree](/wiki/Saddle_tree "Saddle tree") that provides support to horse and rider when working long hours in the saddle. The western saddle features a prominent pommel topped by a horn (a knob used for dallying a [lariat](/wiki/Lasso "Lasso") after roping an animal), wide [stirrups](/wiki/Stirrup "Stirrup"), and in some cases, both front and back cinches. The depth of the seat may depend on the activity, a deeper seat used for [barrel racing](/wiki/Barrel_racing "Barrel racing") or [cutting cows](/wiki/Cutting_(sport) "Cutting (sport)") or a more shallow seat for general ranch riding or [Steer wrestling](/wiki/Steer_wrestling "Steer wrestling").

Finished western horses are asked to perform with a loose rein controlled by one hand. The headstall of a western bridle may utilize either a [Snaffle bit](/wiki/Snaffle_bit "Snaffle bit") or [curb bit](/wiki/Curb_bit "Curb bit"). Bitless headstalls are also seen, such as a [bosal](/wiki/Bosal "Bosal")-style [hackamore](/wiki/Hackamore "Hackamore") on younger horses, or various styles of [mechanical hackamore](/wiki/Mechanical_hackamore "Mechanical hackamore"). In [Vaquero style training](/wiki/Vaquero "Vaquero"), a combination of a bosal and bit, called a "two-rein", is used at some stages of training. The standard western bridle lacks a [noseband](/wiki/Noseband "Noseband") and usually consists of a single set of reins attached to a [curb bit](/wiki/Curb_bit "Curb bit") that has somewhat longer [shanks](/wiki/Bit_shank "Bit shank") than the curb of an English [Weymouth](/wiki/Double_bridle "Double bridle") bridle or a [pelham bit](/wiki/Pelham_bit "Pelham bit"). Western bridles have either a [browband](/wiki/Bridle#Parts "Bridle") or else a "one ear" loop (sometimes two) that crosses in front of the horse's ear. Two styles of Western [reins](/wiki/Rein "Rein") developed: The long split reins of the Texas tradition, which are completely separated, or the "[Romal](/wiki/Romal "Romal")" reins of the California tradition, which are closed reins with a long single attachment (the romal) that can be used as a [quirt](/wiki/Quirt "Quirt"). Modern [rodeo](/wiki/Rodeo "Rodeo") competitors in timed events sometimes use a closed rein without a *romal*.

Western riders wear a long-sleeved shirt, long pants or jeans, [cowboy boots](/wiki/Cowboy_boots "Cowboy boots"), and a wide-brimmed [cowboy hat](/wiki/Cowboy_hat "Cowboy hat"). A rider may wear protective leather leggings called [chaps](/wiki/Chaps "Chaps"). Riders may wear brighter colors or finer fabrics in competition than for work. In particular, horse show events such as [Western pleasure](/wiki/Western_pleasure "Western pleasure") may much flashier equipment. Saddles, bits and bridles are ornamented with substantial amounts of silver, rider clothing may have vivid colors and even rhinestones or sequins.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Harness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Harness")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Welsh-A-Buggy.jpg/250px-Welsh-A-Buggy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Welsh-A-Buggy.jpg)

A Welsh pony in fine harness competition

Main article: [Driving (horse)](/wiki/Driving_(horse) "Driving (horse)")

Horses, [ponies](/wiki/Ponies "Ponies"), [mules](/wiki/Mule "Mule") and [donkeys](/wiki/Donkey "Donkey") are driven in [harness](/wiki/Horse_harness "Horse harness") in many different ways. For working purposes, they can pull a [plow](/wiki/Plow "Plow") or other [farm](/wiki/Farm "Farm") equipment designed to be pulled by animals. In many parts of the world they still pull [wagons](/wiki/Wagon "Wagon") for basic hauling and transportation. They may draw [carriages](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage") at ceremonies, in parades or for tourist rides.

As noted in "horse racing" *above*, horses can race in harness, pulling a very lightweight cart known as a [sulky](/wiki/Sulky "Sulky"). At the other end of the spectrum, some [draft horses](/wiki/Draft_horse "Draft horse") compete in [horse pulling](/wiki/Horse_pulling "Horse pulling") competitions, where single or teams of horses and their drivers vie to determine who can pull the most weight for a short distance.

In [horse show](/wiki/Horse_show "Horse show") competition, the following general categories of competition are seen:

* [Combined driving](/wiki/Combined_driving "Combined driving"), an internationally recognized competition where horses perform an arena-based "dressage" class where precision and control are emphasized, a cross-country "marathon" section that emphasizes fitness and endurance, and a "stadium" or "cones" obstacle course.
* [Draft horse showing](/wiki/Draft_horse_showing "Draft horse showing"): Most [draft horse](/wiki/Draft_horse "Draft horse") performance competition is done in harness.
* [Pleasure driving](/wiki/Pleasure_driving "Pleasure driving"): Horses and ponies are usually hitched to a light cart shown at a walk and two speeds of trot, with an emphasis on manners.
* [Fine harness](/wiki/Fine_harness "Fine harness"): Also called "Formal driving", Horses are hitched to a light four-wheeled cart and shown in a manner that emphasizes flashy action and dramatic performance.
* [Roadster](/wiki/Roadster_(horse) "Roadster (horse)"): A horse show competition where exhibitors wear [racing silks](/wiki/Racing_silks "Racing silks") and ride in a sulky in a style akin to [harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing"), only without actually racing, but rather focusing on manners and performance.
* [Carriage](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage") driving, using somewhat larger two or four wheeled carriages, often restored [antiques](/wiki/Antique "Antique"), judged on the turnout/neatness or suitability of horse and carriage.

## Rodeo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Rodeo")]

Main article: [Rodeo](/wiki/Rodeo "Rodeo")

Rodeo events include the following forms of competition:

### Timed events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Timed events")]

* [Barrel racing](/wiki/Barrel_racing "Barrel racing") and [pole bending](/wiki/Pole_bending "Pole bending") – the timed speed and agility events seen in [rodeo](/wiki/Rodeo "Rodeo") as well as [gymkhana](/wiki/Gymkhana_(Equestrian) "Gymkhana (Equestrian)") or [O-Mok-See](/wiki/Gymkhana_(Equestrian) "Gymkhana (Equestrian)") competition. Both men and women compete in speed events at gymkhanas or O-Mok-Sees; however, at most professional, sanctioned rodeos, barrel racing is an exclusively women's sport. In a barrel race, horse and rider gallop around a cloverleaf pattern of barrels, making agile turns without knocking the barrels over. In pole bending, horse and rider run the length of a line of six upright poles, turn sharply and weave through the poles, turn again and weave back, then return to the start.
* [Steer wrestling](/wiki/Steer_wrestling "Steer wrestling") – Also known as "Bulldogging", this is a rodeo event where the rider jumps off his horse onto a steer and 'wrestles' it to the ground by grabbing it by the horns. This is probably the single most physically dangerous event in rodeo for the cowboy, who runs a high risk of jumping off a running horse head first and missing the steer or of having the thrown steer land on top of him, sometimes horns first.
* [Goat tying](/wiki/Goat_tying "Goat tying") – usually an event for women or pre-teen girls and boys, a goat is staked out while a mounted rider runs to the goat, dismounts, grabs the goat, throws it to the ground and ties it in the same manner as a calf. This event was designed to teach smaller or younger riders the basics of calf roping without the more complex need to also lasso the animal.

### Roping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Roping")]

Roping includes a number of timed events that are based on the real-life tasks of a working cowboy, who often had to capture calves and adult [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle") for [branding](/wiki/Livestock_branding "Livestock branding"), medical treatment and other purposes. A lasso or [lariat](/wiki/Lasso "Lasso") is thrown over the head of a [calf](/wiki/Calf_(animal) "Calf (animal)") or the horns of adult cattle, and the animal is secured in a fashion dictated by its size and age.

* [Calf roping](/wiki/Calf_roping "Calf roping"), also called "tie-down roping", is an event where a calf is roped around the neck by a [lariat](/wiki/Lasso "Lasso"), the horse stops and sets back on the rope while the cowboy dismounts, runs to the calf, throws it to the ground and ties three feet together. (If the horse throws the calf, the cowboy must lose time waiting for the calf to get back to its feet so that the cowboy can do the work. The job of the horse is to hold the calf steady on the rope) This activity is still practiced on modern working ranches for [branding](/wiki/Brand "Brand"), medical treatment, and so on.
* [Team roping](/wiki/Team_roping "Team roping"), also called "heading and heeling", is the only rodeo event where men and women riders may compete together. Two people capture and restrain a full-grown steer. One horse and rider, the "header", lassos a running steer's horns, while the other horse and rider, the "heeler", lassos the steer's two hind legs. Once the animal is captured, the riders face each other and lightly pull the steer between them, so that it loses its balance, thus in the real world allowing restraint for treatment.
* [Breakaway roping](/wiki/Breakaway_roping "Breakaway roping") – an easier form of calf roping where a very short lariat is used, tied lightly to the saddle horn with string and a flag. When the calf is roped, the horse stops, allowing the calf to run on, flagging the end of time when the string and flag breaks from the saddle. In the United States, this event is primarily for women of all ages and boys under 12, while in some nations where traditional calf roping is frowned upon, riders of both genders compete.

### "Rough Stock" competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: \"Rough Stock\" competition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Horses_abreast_IMG_5342.jpg/250px-Horses_abreast_IMG_5342.jpg)](/wiki/File:Horses_abreast_IMG_5342.jpg)

Small herd of rough stock in Texas

In spite of popular myth, most modern "broncs" are not in fact wild horses,[[29]](#cite_note-29) but horses bred specifically as bucking stock.

* [Bronc riding](/wiki/Bronc_riding "Bronc riding") – there are two divisions in rodeo, [bareback bronc riding](/wiki/Bronc_riding#Bareback_bronc_vs._saddle_bronc_riding "Bronc riding"), where the rider rides a [bucking horse](/wiki/Bucking_horse "Bucking horse") holding onto a leather [surcingle](/wiki/Surcingle "Surcingle") or rigging with only one hand, and [saddle bronc riding](/wiki/Bronc_riding#Bareback_bronc_vs._saddle_bronc_riding "Bronc riding"), where the rider rides a modified western saddle without a horn (for safety) while holding onto a braided lead rope attached to the horse's halter.
* [Bull riding](/wiki/Bull_riding "Bull riding") – though technically not an equestrian event, as the cowboys ride full-grown [bucking bulls](/wiki/Bucking_bull "Bucking bull") instead of horses, skills similar to bareback bronc riding are required.

## Other equestrian activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other equestrian activities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/PoloGirlsHorses.jpg/250px-PoloGirlsHorses.jpg)](/wiki/File:PoloGirlsHorses.jpg)

Girls and their horses preparing for a [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo") game

There are many other forms of equestrian activity and sports seen worldwide. There are both competitive events and [pleasure riding](/wiki/Pleasure_riding "Pleasure riding") disciplines available.

### Arena sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Arena sports")]

* Arena [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo") and [Cowboy polo](/wiki/Cowboy_polo "Cowboy polo")
* [Pato](/wiki/Pato "Pato") (Argentina's national sport)
* [Equestrian vaulting](/wiki/Equestrian_vaulting "Equestrian vaulting"): In vaulting, a [surcingle](/wiki/Surcingle "Surcingle") with two hoops at the top is attached around a horse's [barrel](/wiki/Horse_anatomy "Horse anatomy"). The horse also wears a [bridle](/wiki/Bridle "Bridle") with [side reins](/wiki/Side_reins "Side reins"). The vaulter is [longed](/wiki/Longeing "Longeing") on the horse, and performs [gymnastic](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics") movements while the horse walks, trots, and canters.
* [Gymkhana](/wiki/Gymkhana_(Equestrian) "Gymkhana (Equestrian)"), competition of timed pattern games, also known as *O-Mok-See* in the western United States.

### Horse sports that use cattle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Horse sports that use cattle")]

* [Bullfighting](/wiki/Bullfighting "Bullfighting") (*[rejoneo](/wiki/Rejoneador "Rejoneador")*)
  + [Portuguese-style bullfighting](/wiki/Portuguese-style_bullfighting "Portuguese-style bullfighting")
  + [Spanish-style bullfighting](/wiki/Spanish-style_bullfighting "Spanish-style bullfighting")
* [Campdrafting](/wiki/Campdrafting "Campdrafting"), a type of cattle-working competition popular in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia")
* [Cutting](/wiki/Cutting_(sport) "Cutting (sport)")
* [Team penning](/wiki/Team_penning "Team penning")
* [Working cow horse](/wiki/Working_cow_horse "Working cow horse")

### Defined area sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Defined area sports")]

* [Buzkashi](/wiki/Buzkashi "Buzkashi"), a sport originating on the [steppes](/wiki/Steppes "Steppes") of central [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), now the national sport of [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan").
* [Cowboy mounted shooting](/wiki/Cowboy_mounted_shooting "Cowboy mounted shooting")
* [Horseball](/wiki/Horseball "Horseball")
* [Jousting](/wiki/Jousting "Jousting") and [Skill at Arms](/w/index.php?title=Skill_at_Arms&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skill at Arms (page does not exist)"), events involving use of lances, swords and completion of obstacles. There are stand-alone competitions and also are often seen at [historical reenactments](/wiki/Historical_reenactment "Historical reenactment"), [Renaissance Fairs](/wiki/Renaissance_Fair "Renaissance Fair") and [Society for Creative Anachronism](/wiki/Society_for_Creative_Anachronism "Society for Creative Anachronism") events.
* [Mounted archery](/wiki/Mounted_archery "Mounted archery")
  + [Yabusame](/wiki/Yabusame "Yabusame")
* [Mounted Games](/wiki/Mounted_Games "Mounted Games"), a sport where games are played in a relay-style with two to five members per team at very high speed
* [Polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo"), a team game played on horses, involves riders using a long-handled [mallet](/wiki/Mallet "Mallet") to drive a ball on the ground into the opposing team's [goal](/wiki/Goal_(sport) "Goal (sport)") while the opposing team defends their goal
* [Polocrosse](/wiki/Polocrosse "Polocrosse")
* [Tent pegging](/wiki/Tent_pegging "Tent pegging")

### Cross-country sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cross-country sports")]

* [Competitive Mounted Orienteering](/wiki/Competitive_Mounted_Orienteering "Competitive Mounted Orienteering"), a form of orienteering on horses (but unrelated to [orienteering](/wiki/Orienteering "Orienteering")) – consists of three stages: following a precise route marked on a map, negotiation of obstacles and control of paces.
* [Le Trec](/wiki/Techniques_de_Randonn%C3%A9e_%C3%89questre_de_Comp%C3%A9tition "Techniques de Randonnée Équestre de Compétition"), which comprises three phases – trail riding, with jumping and correct basic flatwork. Le Trec, which is very popular in Europe, tests the partnership's ability to cope with an all-day ride across varied terrain, route finding, negotiating natural obstacles and hazards, while considering the welfare of the horse, respecting the countryside and enjoying all it has to offer.
* [Competitive trail riding](/wiki/Competitive_trail_riding "Competitive trail riding"), a [pace race](/wiki/Pace_race "Pace race") held across terrain similar to [endurance riding](/wiki/Endurance_riding "Endurance riding"), but shorter in length (25 – 35 miles (56 km), depending on class). Being a form of [pace race](/wiki/Pace_race "Pace race"), the objective is not to finish in the least time. Instead, as in other forms of [judged trail riding](/wiki/Judged_trail_ride "Judged trail ride"), each competitor is graded on everything including physical condition, campsite and horse management. Horsemanship also is considered, including how the rider handles the trail and how horse is handled and presented to the judge and vet throughout the ride. The horse is graded on performance, manners, etc. "Pulse and respiration" stops check the horse's recovery ability. The judges also set up obstacles along the trail and the horse and rider are graded on how well they perform as a team. The whole point is the partnership between the horse and rider.
* [Cross Country Jumping](/wiki/Cross_Country_Jumping "Cross Country Jumping"), a jumping course that contains logs and natural obstacles mostly. The common clothes worn are usually brighter colors and less conservative.
* [Endurance riding](/wiki/Endurance_riding "Endurance riding"), a competition usually of 50 to 100 miles (160 km) or more, over mountainous or other natural terrain, with scheduled stops to take the horses' vital signs, check soundness and verify that the horse is fit to continue. The first horse to finish and be confirmed by the veterinarian as fit to continue is the winner. Additional awards are usually given to the best-conditioned horses who finish in the top 10.
* [Fox hunting](/wiki/Fox_hunting "Fox hunting")
* Hacking, or [pleasure riding](/wiki/Pleasure_riding "Pleasure riding").
* [Hunter Pacing](/wiki/Hunter_Pacing "Hunter Pacing") is a sport where a horse and rider team travel a trail at speeds based the ideal conditions for the horse, with competitors seeking to ride closest to that perfect time. Hunter paces are usually held in a series. Hunter paces are usually a few miles long and covered mostly at a canter or gallop. The horsemanship and management skills of the rider are also considered in the scoring, and periodic stops are required for veterinarians to check the vital signs and overall soundness of the horses.
* [Ride and Tie](/wiki/Ride_and_Tie "Ride and Tie") is a form of [endurance riding](/wiki/Endurance_riding "Endurance riding") in which teams of 3 (two humans and one horse) alternate running and riding.
* [Steeplechase](/wiki/Steeplechase_(horse_racing) "Steeplechase (horse racing)"), a distance horse race with diverse fence and ditch obstacles.
* [Trail Riding](/wiki/Trail_Riding "Trail Riding"), pleasure riding any breed horse, any style across the land.

## Health issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Health issues")]

Horseback riding is a leading cause of sports-related traumatic brain injury in the United States. A 2023 study of 210 equestrians found a higher incidence of concussion in equestrian sports than football or rugby.

Handling, riding and driving horses have inherent risks. Horses are large prey animals with a well-developed [flight or fight instinct](/wiki/Flight_or_fight_instinct "Flight or fight instinct") able to move quickly and unexpectedly. When mounted, the rider's head may be up to 4 m (13 ft) from the ground, and the horse may travel at a speed of up to 65 km/h (40 mph).[[30]](#cite_note-30) The injuries observed range from very minor injuries to fatalities.

A study in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") reported that the [relative risk](/wiki/Relative_risk "Relative risk") of injury from riding a horse, compared to riding a bicycle, was 9 times higher for [adolescents](/wiki/Adolescent "Adolescent") and 5.6 times higher for younger [children](/wiki/Child "Child"), but that riding a horse was less risky than riding a [moped](/wiki/Moped "Moped").[[31]](#cite_note-pmid17426559-31) In [Victoria, Australia](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia"), a search of state records found that equestrian sports had the third highest incidence of serious injury, after [motor sports](/wiki/Motor_sport "Motor sport") and [power boating](/wiki/Power_boat "Power boat").[[32]](#cite_note-pmid16046347-32) In [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), an analysis of a national registry estimated the incidence of equestrian injury to be 21 per 100,000 person-years for farming and equestrian sports combined, and 160 times higher for [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") personnel. Other findings noted that helmets likely prevent traumatic brain injuries.[[33]](#cite_note-pmid15128138-33)

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") each year an estimated 30 million people ride horses, resulting in 50,000 [emergency department](/wiki/Emergency_department "Emergency department") visits (1 visit per 600 riders per year).[[34]](#cite_note-pmid17461318-34) A survey of 679 equestrians in Oregon, Washington and Idaho estimated that at some time in their equestrian career one in five will be seriously injured, resulting in hospitalization, surgery or long-term disability.[[35]](#cite_note-pmid17414356-35) Among survey respondents, novice equestrians had an incidence of any injury that was threefold over intermediates, fivefold over advanced equestrians, and nearly eightfold over professionals. Approximately 100 hours of experience are required to achieve a substantial decline in the risk of injury. The survey authors conclude that efforts to prevent equestrian injury should focus on novice equestrians.

### Mechanisms of injury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Mechanisms of injury")]

The most common injury is falling from the horse, followed by being kicked, trampled and bitten. About 3 out of 4 injuries are due to falling, broadly defined.[[36]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-36)[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) A broad definition of falling often includes being crushed and being thrown from the horse, but when reported separately each of these mechanisms may be more common than being kicked.[[38]](#cite_note-pmid18695484-38)[[39]](#cite_note-pmid18424291-39)

### Types and severity of injury

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Types and severity of injury")]

In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), a 10-year study of trauma center patients injured while riding reported that although 48% had suffered head injuries, only 9% of these riders had been wearing helmets at the time of their accident. Other injuries involved the chest (54%), abdomen (22%) and extremities (17%).[[40]](#cite_note-ball-2007-40) A German study reported that injuries in horse riding are rare compared to other sports, but when they occur they are severe. Specifically, they found that 40% of horse riding injuries were fractures, and only 15% were sprains. Furthermore, the study noted that in Germany, one quarter of all sport related fatalities are caused by horse riding.[[41]](#cite_note-pmid1793946-41)
Most horse related injuries are a result of falling from a horse, which is the cause of 60–80% of all such reported injuries.[[36]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-36)[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) Another common cause of injury is being kicked by a horse, which may cause skull fractures or severe trauma to the [internal organs](/wiki/Internal_organs "Internal organs"). Some possible injuries resulting from horse riding, with the percent indicating the amounts in relation to all injuries as reported by a [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") study,[[42]](#cite_note-pmid14581953-42) include:

* Arm fracture or dislocation (31%)
* Head injury (21%)
* Leg fracture or dislocation (15%)
* Chest injury (33%)

Among 36 members and employees of the [Hong Kong Jockey Club](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Jockey_Club "Hong Kong Jockey Club") who were seen in a trauma center during a period of 5 years, 24 fell from horses and 11 were kicked by the horse. Injuries comprised: 18 torso; 11 head, face or neck; and 11 limb.[[43]](#cite_note-pmid17049524-43) The authors of this study recommend that helmets, face shields and body protectors be worn when riding or handling horses.

In [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), a study of equestrians seen at one hospital over a 6-year period found that helmet use both increased over time and was correlated with a lower rate of admission. However, 81% of admissions were wearing a helmet at the time of injury,[[44]](#cite_note-pmid12887517-44) In the second half of the study period, of the equestrians seen at a hospital, only 14% were admitted. In contrast, a study of child equestrians seen at a hospital emergency department in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide") reported that 60% were admitted.[[45]](#cite_note-pmid18782209-45)

In the United States, an analysis of [National Electronic Injury Surveillance System](/w/index.php?title=National_Electronic_Injury_Surveillance_System&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Electronic Injury Surveillance System (page does not exist)") (NEISS) data performed by the Equestrian Medical Safety Association studied 78,279 horse-related injuries in 2007: "The most common injuries included fractures (28.5%); contusions/abrasions (28.3%); strain/sprain (14.5%); internal injury (8.1%); lacerations (5.7%); concussions (4.6%); dislocations (1.9%); and hematomas (1.2%). Most frequent injury sites are the lower trunk (19.6%); head (15.0%); upper trunk (13.4%); shoulder (8.2%); and wrist (6.8%). Within this study patients were treated and released (86.2%), were hospitalized (8.7%), were transferred (3.6%), left without being treated (0.8%), remained for observation (0.6%) and arrived at the hospital deceased (0.1%)."[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Head injuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Head injuries")]

Horseback riding is one of the most dangerous sports, especially in relation to head injury. Statistics from the United States, for example, indicate that about 30 million people ride horses annually.[[47]](#cite_note-47) On average, about 67,000 people are admitted to the hospital each year from injuries sustained while working with horses.[[48]](#cite_note-48) 15,000 of those admittances are from traumatic brain injuries. Of those, about 60 die each year from their brain injuries.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Studies have found horseback riding to be more dangerous than several sports, including skiing, auto racing and football.[[40]](#cite_note-ball-2007-40) Horseback riding has a higher hospital admittance rate per hours of riding than motorcycle racing, at 0.49 per thousand hours of riding and 0.14 accidents per thousand hours, respectively.[[40]](#cite_note-ball-2007-40)

Head injuries are especially traumatic in horseback riding. About two-thirds of all riders requiring hospitalization after a fall have sustained a traumatic brain injury.[[50]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-50) Falling from a horse without wearing a helmet is comparable to being struck by a car.[[51]](#cite_note-Nelson,_M.A._1992-51) Most falling deaths are caused by head injury.[[51]](#cite_note-Nelson,_M.A._1992-51)

The use of riding helmets substantially decreases the likelihood and severity of head injuries. Riders falling with a helmet are five times less likely to experience a traumatic brain injury than a rider who falls without a helmet.[[50]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-50) Helmets work by crushing on impact and extending the length of time it takes the head to stop moving.[[39]](#cite_note-pmid18424291-39) Despite this, helmet usage rates in North America are estimated to be between eight and twenty percent.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Once a helmet has sustained an impact from falling, that part of the helmet is structurally weakened, even if no visible damage is present.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Helmet manufacturers recommend that a helmet that has undergone impact from a fall be replaced immediately. In addition, helmets should be replaced every three to five years; specific recommendations vary by manufacturer.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Rules on helmet use in competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Rules on helmet use in competition")]

Many organizations mandate helmet use in competition or on show grounds, and rules have continually moved in the direction of requiring helmet use. In 2011, the United States Equestrian Federation passed a rule making helmet use mandatory while mounted on competition grounds at U.S. nationally rated eventing competitions.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Also in 2011, the United States Dressage Federation made helmet use in competition mandatory for all riders under 18 and all riders who are riding any test at Fourth Level and below.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Riders competing at Prix St. Georges and above and also riding a test at Fourth Level or below, must also wear a helmet at all times while mounted.

### Riding astride

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equestrianism&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Riding astride")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Ridderstedt_couple_on_horseback.jpg/250px-Ridderstedt_couple_on_horseback.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ridderstedt_couple_on_horseback.jpg)

By the 1930s and 1940s most horse riding had become occasional and leisurely or competitive rather than being the common method of transportation it had been for centuries before

The idea that riding a horse astride could injure a woman's sex organs is a historic, but sometimes popular even today, misunderstanding or misconception, particularly that riding astride can damage the [hymen](/wiki/Hymen "Hymen").[[57]](#cite_note-pmid12346860-57) Evidence of injury to any female sex organs is scant. In female high-level athletes, trauma to the [perineum](/wiki/Perineum "Perineum") is rare and is associated with certain sports (see [Pelvic floor#Clinical significance](/wiki/Pelvic_floor#Clinical_significance "Pelvic floor")). The type of trauma associated with [equestrian sports](/wiki/Equestrian_sports "Equestrian sports") has been termed "horse riders' perineum".[[58]](#cite_note-pmid17450681-58) A [case series](/wiki/Case_series "Case series") of 4 female mountain bike riders and 2 female horse riders found both patient-reported perineal pain and evidence of [sub-clinical](/wiki/Sub-clinical "Sub-clinical") changes in the [clitoris](/wiki/Clitoris "Clitoris");[[59]](#cite_note-59) the relevance of these findings to horse riding is unknown.

In men, sports-related injuries are among the major causes of testicular trauma. In a small [controlled](/wiki/Scientific_control "Scientific control") but [unblinded](/wiki/Blind_experiment "Blind experiment") study of 52 men, [varicocele](/wiki/Varicocele "Varicocele") was significantly more common in equestrians than in non-equestrians.[[60]](#cite_note-pmid15972705-60) The difference between these two groups was small, however, compared to differences reported between extreme [mountain bike](/wiki/Mountain_bike "Mountain bike") riders and non-riders,[[61]](#cite_note-pmid11323467-61) and also between mountain bike riders and on-road bicycle riders.[[62]](#cite_note-pmid18185039-62) Horse-riding injuries to the [scrotum](/wiki/Scrotum "Scrotum") ([contusions](/wiki/Contusion "Contusion")) and [testes](/wiki/Testes "Testes") ([blunt trauma](/wiki/Blunt_trauma "Blunt trauma")) were well known to surgeons in the 19th century and early 20th century.[[63]](#cite_note-KeenDaCosta1908-63) Injuries from collision with the pommel of a saddle are mentioned specifically.[[63]](#cite_note-KeenDaCosta1908-63)
